title: ROBOTICS HANDBOOK
text:
Concepts and mathematics from robotics, SLAM, and localization — written the way I wish someone had explained them to me.

title: Overview
text:
Here’s the core problem SLAM is trying to solve: given thousands of noisy sensor measurements, how do we estimate both the robot’s trajectory and the map it’s moving through?
The pipeline looks like this:
Sensors → Measurements → Factor Graph → Probability → MAP Estimation → Least Squares → Sparse Matrix Solver → Best robot pose + map
Every arrow in that chain is doing real work, and we’ll unpack each one as we go.

title: Overview
text:
Here’s the core problem SLAM is trying to solve: given thousands of noisy sensor measurements, how do we estimate both the robot’s trajectory and the map it’s moving through?
The pipeline looks like this:
Sensors → Measurements → Factor Graph → Probability → MAP Estimation → Least Squares → Sparse Matrix Solver → Best robot pose + map
Every arrow in that chain is doing real work, and we’ll unpack each one as we go.

title: Overview
text:
2 The Two Halves of SLAM
SLAM naturally splits into two subsystems that do very different jobs:



Frontend
Backend




Extracts features
Receives constraints


ICP correspondences
Creates the graph


Scan matching
Optimization


Visual features
Trajectory estimation


Loop closure detection



Data association




Think of the frontend as perception — turning raw sensor data into usable geometric relationships — and the backend as inference — taking those relationships and solving for the most consistent trajectory and map.
The historical arc of how we solve the backend:
\[
\text{EKF SLAM} \rightarrow \text{Square Root SLAM} \rightarrow \text{Factor Graph SLAM}
\]

title: Least Squares & MAP
text:
Here’s the reframing that makes SLAM computationally tractable:

What is the most probable robot trajectory? is exactly equivalent to minimize the sum of squared errors.

That equivalence falls directly out of assuming Gaussian noise — we’ll derive it properly in a later section. For now, here’s the pipeline it sets up:
Measurements → Posterior → MAP estimate → Gaussian probability → negative log → Least squares → Gauss-Newton / Levenberg-Marquardt


The unknowns are the full state vector, and the measurements are whatever the sensors actually gave us:
\[
x = \begin{bmatrix} P_1 \\ P_2 \\ \vdots \\ l_1 \\ l_2 \\ \vdots \end{bmatrix}
\qquad
z = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_m\}
\]
The goal: find the best value of \(x\) given \(z\) — in other words, maximize \(p(x \mid z)\).

title: Least Squares & MAP
text:
Here’s the reframing that makes SLAM computationally tractable:

What is the most probable robot trajectory? is exactly equivalent to minimize the sum of squared errors.

That equivalence falls directly out of assuming Gaussian noise — we’ll derive it properly in a later section. For now, here’s the pipeline it sets up:
Measurements → Posterior → MAP estimate → Gaussian probability → negative log → Least squares → Gauss-Newton / Levenberg-Marquardt


The unknowns are the full state vector, and the measurements are whatever the sensors actually gave us:
\[
x = \begin{bmatrix} P_1 \\ P_2 \\ \vdots \\ l_1 \\ l_2 \\ \vdots \end{bmatrix}
\qquad
z = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_m\}
\]
The goal: find the best value of \(x\) given \(z\) — in other words, maximize \(p(x \mid z)\).

title: Least Squares & MAP
text:
2 MAP Estimation
MAP stands for Maximum A Posteriori — we want the state whose posterior probability is highest:
\[
x_{MAP} = \operatorname*{argmax}_{x} \; p(x \mid z)
\]

2.1 Applying Bayes’ theorem
Directly optimizing \(p(x \mid z)\) is awkward, so we flip it using Bayes’ rule:
\[
x_{MAP} = \operatorname*{argmax}_{x} \;
\underbrace{p(z \mid x)}_{\text{likelihood}} \cdot
\underbrace{p(x)}_{\text{prior}}
\]
These two terms have intuitive meanings:

Likelihood \(p(z \mid x)\) — does this candidate state actually explain the measurements we observed?
Prior \(p(x)\) — is this state physically plausible in the first place?

We can drop \(p(z)\) entirely, since it doesn’t depend on \(x\) — it’s just a normalizing constant.


2.2 Rewriting with factors
Since the posterior already factors into a product of \(\phi_i\) terms (as we saw earlier), this becomes:
\[
x_{MAP} = \operatorname*{argmax}_{x} \; \prod_i \phi_i(x_i)
\]
Nothing has changed mathematically — we’ve just rewritten the same optimization in terms of factors. But this is the general MAP problem, and it’s exactly the form every SLAM backend is actually solving.

title: Least Squares & MAP
text:
3 Modeling Measurement Noise
Every real sensor measurement is a mix of a true underlying signal and noise. We model this as:
\[
z = h(x) + \eta
\]

\(h(x)\) is the prediction function — it comes from pure geometry and physics, with no randomness involved.
\(\eta\) is the noise, assumed Gaussian: \(\eta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma)\).

That Gaussian assumption on the noise is doing a lot of work here — it’s what eventually lets “most probable trajectory” collapse into “minimize sum of squared errors.”
Given this noise model, the probability of observing \(z\) given a state \(x\) is:
\[
p(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(z;\, h(x), \Sigma)
\]
Expanding the Gaussian density explicitly:
\[
p(z \mid x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\Sigma}}
\exp\left(-\frac{1}{2} [z - h(x)]^T \Sigma^{-1} [z - h(x)]\right)
\]

title: Least Squares & MAP
text:
4 Reading the Pieces

\(z - h(x)\) is the residual: actual measurement minus predicted measurement. It’s a direct measure of how wrong our current estimate is.
Inside the exponent we have the squared residual, weighted by \(\Sigma^{-1}\).
\(\Sigma\) embeds the noise — it’s the covariance of the measurement.
\(\Sigma^{-1}\) is called the information matrix, since it tells us how much confidence we place in this measurement (a tighter covariance means more information).

title: Least Squares & MAP
text:
5 The Mahalanobis Distance
The weighted squared residual has a name:
\[
[z - h(x)]^T \Sigma^{-1} [z - h(x)] = \text{Mahalanobis distance squared}
\]
Unlike ordinary Euclidean distance, Mahalanobis distance accounts for how uncertain each dimension of the measurement is — a large deviation in a noisy direction counts for less than the same deviation in a precise direction. We give it its own compact notation:
\[
\|e\|^2_\Sigma = e^T \Sigma^{-1} e
\]
This is exactly the quantity each factor in a SLAM problem is scoring.

title: Least Squares & MAP
text:
6 Putting the Factors Together
Recall the MAP objective in terms of factors:
\[
x_{MAP} = \operatorname*{argmax}_{x} \; \prod_i \phi_i(x_i)
\]
Given the Gaussian noise model, each factor takes the form:
\[
\phi_i(x_i) \propto \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2} \|z_i - h_i(x_i)\|^2_{\Sigma_i}\right)
\]
which we can write more compactly as:
\[
\phi_i(x_i) = c_i \cdot e^{-E_i}
\]
where \(E_i = \frac{1}{2}\|z_i - h_i(x_i)\|^2_{\Sigma_i}\) is the weighted squared error for factor \(i\).

title: Least Squares & MAP
text:
7 The Product Problem
Here’s a practical issue: the MAP objective is a product of many exponential terms:
\[
x_{MAP} \to \exp(-a) \cdot \exp(-b) \cdot \exp(-c) \cdots
\]
Optimizers don’t work well with long products — products of many small numbers underflow numerically, and their derivatives get messy. The fix is to take logarithms, which turns the product into a sum:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\prod_i \exp(-E_i)
\;\longrightarrow\;
& \log\left(\prod_i \exp(-E_i)\right) \\
\;\longrightarrow\;
& \sum_i \log(\exp(-E_i)) \\
\;\longrightarrow\;
& \sum_i (-E_i)
\end{aligned}
\]
Since \(\log\) is monotonic, maximizing the product is equivalent to maximizing the log-sum. And flipping the sign turns a maximization into a minimization:
\[
\operatorname*{argmax}\left(-\sum_i E_i\right)
= \operatorname*{argmin}\left(\sum_i E_i\right)
\]
This is the final piece connecting everything: maximizing the posterior probability is exactly equivalent to minimizing a sum of weighted squared residuals — which is precisely the least-squares objective we started with back in the Least Squares section.

title: Least Squares & MAP
section: 8 From MAP to a Concrete Optimization Problem
text:
8 From MAP to a Concrete Optimization Problem
Taking the MAP formulation and working through the Gaussian noise assumption from the previous section eventually lands us here:
\[
x_{MAP} = \operatorname*{argmin}_{x} \sum_i \| z_i - h_i(x_i) \|^2_{\Sigma_i}
\quad \to \quad \text{Least squares}
\]
This is a least squares problem. But there’s a catch: \(h_i(x)\) — the function that predicts a measurement from a state — is generally a non-linear function of the state. Bearing angles, ranges, rotations: none of these are linear in \(x\). So what we actually have is a non-linear least squares problem.

title: Cholesky & QR
text:
We’re left needing to solve the normal equations:
\[
A^T A \, \delta = A^T b
\]
Instead of inverting \(A^T A\) directly (expensive and numerically fragile), we factor it:
\[
A^T A = R^T R \quad \to \; \text{Cholesky factor}
\]
where \(R\) and \(R^T\) are triangular matrices. Triangular systems are cheap to solve — that’s the whole motivation here.
Substituting back into the normal equations:
\[
R^T R \, \delta = A^T b
\]
Rather than solving this all at once, we split it into two much easier triangular systems:

Solve for \(y\) using \(R^T y = A^T b\)
Then solve for \(\delta\) using \(R\delta = y\)

Each of these is a simple triangular solve — no expensive inversion required.






TipThe payoff



Cholesky takes the computation from \(O(n^3)\) (a direct inversion) down to \(O(n^2)\) per solve, by replacing one hard problem with two cheap triangular ones.

title: Cholesky & QR
text:
We’re left needing to solve the normal equations:
\[
A^T A \, \delta = A^T b
\]
Instead of inverting \(A^T A\) directly (expensive and numerically fragile), we factor it:
\[
A^T A = R^T R \quad \to \; \text{Cholesky factor}
\]
where \(R\) and \(R^T\) are triangular matrices. Triangular systems are cheap to solve — that’s the whole motivation here.
Substituting back into the normal equations:
\[
R^T R \, \delta = A^T b
\]
Rather than solving this all at once, we split it into two much easier triangular systems:

Solve for \(y\) using \(R^T y = A^T b\)
Then solve for \(\delta\) using \(R\delta = y\)

Each of these is a simple triangular solve — no expensive inversion required.






TipThe payoff



Cholesky takes the computation from \(O(n^3)\) (a direct inversion) down to \(O(n^2)\) per solve, by replacing one hard problem with two cheap triangular ones.

title: Cholesky & QR
text:
2 QR Factorization
Cholesky factors \(A^T A\). QR takes a different approach — it factors \(A\) itself:
\[
A = QR
\]
where \(Q\) is orthogonal and \(R\) is upper triangular.
Orthogonal means \(Q^T Q = I\), which immediately gives us \(Q^{-1} = Q^T\) — the inverse of \(Q\) is just its transpose, essentially free to compute.

2.1 Deriving the Simplified System
Starting from the original system \(A\delta = b\), substitute \(A = QR\):
\[
QR\delta = b
\]
Multiply both sides by \(Q^T\):
\[
Q^T QR\delta = Q^T b
\]
Since \(Q^T Q = I\), this collapses to:
\[
R\delta = Q^T b
\]
This is much simpler to solve — \(R\) is triangular, so it’s just a back-substitution away from \(\delta\).

title: Cholesky & QR
section: 3 Cholesky vs. QR: Same Goal, Different Routes
text:
3 Cholesky vs. QR: Same Goal, Different Routes
Both methods are ultimately solving the same problem, just via different factorizations:



Method
Route
Ever forms \(A^T A\)?




Cholesky
\(A \to A^T A \to R^T R \to\) solve
Yes


QR
\(A \to QR \to\) solve
No




3.1 Trade-offs









Cholesky
QR




Speed
Faster, especially on sparse matrices
Slower


Numerical stability
More sensitive to error
More stable


Typical use
Default choice for SLAM (systems are almost always sparse)
Preferred when stability matters more than speed









NoteWhy QR is more stable



The gap comes down to what happens to small errors: if \(A\) is only slightly inaccurate, computing \(A^T A\) roughly squares that inaccuracy. QR avoids this entirely, since it never forms \(A^T A\) in the first place.


The practical upshot: QR is more numerically stable but slower, while Cholesky is faster — especially on sparse problems — but slightly more sensitive to numerical error. Which one gets used often comes down to this trade-off between speed and stability for the specific problem at hand.

title: Cholesky & QR
section: 4 Optimization Algorithms Only Differ in One Thing
text:
4 Optimization Algorithms Only Differ in One Thing
Zooming out from Cholesky and QR — which are both about solving one linear system — it’s worth placing that step inside the bigger picture of how the optimizer decides to move at all.

Optimization algorithms only differ in how they decide to take the next step. Everything else — the problem setup, the goal — stays the same.

The three methods worth knowing are:

Steepest Descent
Gauss-Newton
Levenberg-Marquardt


4.1 The Optimization Problem
All three algorithms are solving the exact same underlying problem. Suppose we have an objective function:
\[
J(x) : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}
\]
where \(x\) is an \(n\)-dimensional vector, and \(J(x)\) is a scalar cost.
The goal:
\[
x^{*} = \operatorname*{argmin}_{x} J(x)
\]
In SLAM specifically, this cost is the sum of squared, whitened residuals we derived earlier:
\[
J(x) = \sum_i \| r_i(x) \|^2_{\Sigma_i}, \qquad r_i(x) = z_i - h_i(x_i)
\]
Every algorithm below is just a different strategy for minimizing this same \(J(x)\).

title: Cholesky & QR
section: 5 First Order vs. Second Order Optimization
text:
5 First Order vs. Second Order Optimization
Every optimization algorithm tries to approximate what \(J(x+\delta)\) looks like near the current estimate \(x\) — essentially asking “if I step in direction \(\delta\), how does the cost change?” There are two common ways to approximate this.

5.1 1st Order: Gradient Only
\[
J(x+\delta) \approx J(x) + \nabla J(x)^T \delta
\]
Only the gradient is used here — this tells us the direction of steepest change, but nothing about how the cost curves.


5.2 2nd Order: Gradient and Curvature
\[
J(x+\delta) \approx J(x) + \nabla J(x)^T \delta + \frac{1}{2}\delta^T H \delta
\]
where \(H = \nabla^2 J(x)\) is the Hessian matrix — it captures curvature, i.e., how the gradient itself is changing.






TipWhy curvature helps



Knowing curvature lets an algorithm make a much better-informed guess about how big a step to take — instead of just which direction to move in.

title: Cholesky & QR
text:
6 Comparing the Algorithms
The differences between these methods come down to whether — and how — they use the Hessian:



Algorithm
Uses gradient
Uses Hessian




Gradient Descent
✓
✗


Gauss-Newton
✓
Approximate


Newton
✓
Exact


Levenberg-Marquardt
✓
Approximate + Damping



A few things worth noticing in this table:

Gradient descent is the cheapest per step, since it ignores curvature entirely — but that also makes it the least informed about how far to step, often leading to slow zig-zagging convergence.
Newton’s method uses the true Hessian and converges fastest in principle, but computing the exact Hessian is often expensive or impractical for large problems like SLAM.
Gauss-Newton is the practical middle ground: it approximates the Hessian as \(H \approx A^T A\) (as we derived in the normal equations), avoiding the cost of computing second derivatives directly. This is exactly the \(A^T A\) we’ve been factoring with Cholesky and QR above.
Levenberg-Marquardt builds on Gauss-Newton by adding a damping term, blending it with gradient descent behavior when the Gauss-Newton step would be unreliable. This is what makes LM more robust in practice, particularly when far from the optimum.







NoteThe throughline



This progression — from no curvature information, to approximate curvature, to approximate-with-safety-net — is really what connects all three algorithms back to the same Taylor expansion framework.

title: About
text: About this site

title: Optimization Methods
text:
Suppose our current estimate is \(x\), and we want to find the update that gets us to \(x + \delta\). Using the first-order Taylor expansion from before:
\[
J(x+\delta) \approx J(x) + \nabla J(x)^T \delta
\]
To minimize the cost, we want \(\nabla J^T \delta\) to be as negative as possible. Suppose the step length is fixed:
\[
\|\delta\| = \alpha
\]
The question becomes: which direction \(\delta\) minimizes \(\nabla J^T \delta\), given that its length is fixed?



Recall that for any two vectors:
\[
a^T b = \|a\| \|b\| \cos\theta
\]
Applying this to our gradient term:
\[
\nabla J^T \delta = \|\nabla J\| \|\delta\| \cos\theta
\]
and we know \(\|\delta\| = \alpha\) is fixed. So this expression is minimized (most negative) when \(\cos\theta = -1\), i.e. \(\theta = 180°\) — meaning \(\delta\) points in the exact opposite direction of the gradient.
This gives us:
\[
\delta = -\alpha \frac{\nabla J}{\|\nabla J\|}
\]
Absorbing the normalization into \(\alpha\) (since \(\alpha\) was just a free step-size parameter anyway):






ImportantGradient Descent



\[
\delta = -\alpha \nabla J
\]
The intuition matches the name exactly: always step directly downhill, opposite the gradient.











WarningTwo failure modes




Near the minimum, \(\nabla J \to 0\), so the steps themselves shrink to nearly nothing — convergence crawls to a halt right when we’re closest to the answer.
In elongated valleys — where the slope in one direction is much steeper than in another — the algorithm zig-zags back and forth across the steep direction while making painfully slow progress along the shallow one.






Recall the quadratic cost from the normal equations derivation, \(J(\delta) = \|A\delta - b\|^2\). Its gradient is:
\[
g = \nabla J = A^T(Ax - b)
\]
So the gradient descent update becomes:
\[
x_{k+1} = x_k - \alpha A^T (Ax_k - b)
\]
Simple to compute, but — as we’re about to see — far from the most efficient option available.

title: Optimization Methods
text:
Suppose our current estimate is \(x\), and we want to find the update that gets us to \(x + \delta\). Using the first-order Taylor expansion from before:
\[
J(x+\delta) \approx J(x) + \nabla J(x)^T \delta
\]
To minimize the cost, we want \(\nabla J^T \delta\) to be as negative as possible. Suppose the step length is fixed:
\[
\|\delta\| = \alpha
\]
The question becomes: which direction \(\delta\) minimizes \(\nabla J^T \delta\), given that its length is fixed?



Recall that for any two vectors:
\[
a^T b = \|a\| \|b\| \cos\theta
\]
Applying this to our gradient term:
\[
\nabla J^T \delta = \|\nabla J\| \|\delta\| \cos\theta
\]
and we know \(\|\delta\| = \alpha\) is fixed. So this expression is minimized (most negative) when \(\cos\theta = -1\), i.e. \(\theta = 180°\) — meaning \(\delta\) points in the exact opposite direction of the gradient.
This gives us:
\[
\delta = -\alpha \frac{\nabla J}{\|\nabla J\|}
\]
Absorbing the normalization into \(\alpha\) (since \(\alpha\) was just a free step-size parameter anyway):






ImportantGradient Descent



\[
\delta = -\alpha \nabla J
\]
The intuition matches the name exactly: always step directly downhill, opposite the gradient.











WarningTwo failure modes




Near the minimum, \(\nabla J \to 0\), so the steps themselves shrink to nearly nothing — convergence crawls to a halt right when we’re closest to the answer.
In elongated valleys — where the slope in one direction is much steeper than in another — the algorithm zig-zags back and forth across the steep direction while making painfully slow progress along the shallow one.






Recall the quadratic cost from the normal equations derivation, \(J(\delta) = \|A\delta - b\|^2\). Its gradient is:
\[
g = \nabla J = A^T(Ax - b)
\]
So the gradient descent update becomes:
\[
x_{k+1} = x_k - \alpha A^T (Ax_k - b)
\]
Simple to compute, but — as we’re about to see — far from the most efficient option available.

title: Optimization Methods
text:
2 Newton’s Method

2.1 Using Curvature, Not Just Slope
This time we use the full second-order Taylor expansion:
\[
J(x+\delta) = J(x) + g^T \delta + \frac{1}{2}\delta^T H \delta
\]
where \(g = \nabla J\) and \(H = \nabla^2 J\).
To find the minimum, differentiate with respect to \(\delta\) and set the result to zero:
\[
g + H\delta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta = -H^{-1} g
\]






ImportantNewton’s Method



\[
\delta = -H^{-1} g
\]
Unlike gradient descent, this uses curvature — the true Hessian \(\nabla^2 J\) — rather than just the slope. That extra information is what lets Newton’s method converge dramatically faster near the optimum: it doesn’t just know which way is downhill, it knows how the slope itself is changing, so it can take a much better-sized step.




2.2 Why We Don’t Actually Use Newton’s Method






WarningThe catch



Computing the true Hessian is very expensive, especially for problems with thousands of unknowns like SLAM. So instead, we look for a way to approximate the Hessian — which is exactly what Gauss-Newton does.

title: Optimization Methods
text:
3 Gauss-Newton
Recall the cost function in terms of the whitened residual:
\[
J(x) = r(x)^T \Sigma^{-1} r(x), \qquad r(x) = z - h(x)
\]

3.1 Step 1: Linearize
\[
r(x + \delta) \approx r(x) + J_x \delta, \qquad J_x = \frac{\partial r}{\partial x}
\]


3.2 Step 2: Substitute Into the Cost
\[
J(\delta) = (r + J_x \delta)^T \Sigma^{-1} (r + J_x \delta)
\]


3.3 Step 3: Expand
\[
J(\delta) = r^T \Sigma^{-1} r + 2\delta^T J_x^T \Sigma^{-1} r + \delta^T J_x^T \Sigma^{-1} J_x \delta
\]


3.4 Step 4: Differentiate and Set to Zero
Differentiating with respect to \(\delta\) and setting the result to zero gives:
\[
J_x^T \Sigma^{-1} J_x \, \delta = -J_x^T \Sigma^{-1} r
\]
This is the Gauss-Newton equation — mathematically correct as-is, but usually rewritten in a cleaner form.


3.5 Step 5: Whitening (Removing \(\Sigma\))
Define:
\[
A = \Sigma^{-\frac{1}{2}} J, \qquad b = \Sigma^{-\frac{1}{2}} r
\]
so that:
\[
A^T A = J^T \Sigma^{-1} J, \qquad A^T b = J^T \Sigma^{-1} r
\]
Substituting these in gives the familiar form:






ImportantThe Gauss-Newton Update



\[
A^T A \, \delta = -A^T b
\]
This is exactly the normal equations from before — the form most widely used in practice, including in libraries like GTSAM, and the same system solved via Cholesky or QR factorization.




3.6 The Key Insight: We Never Computed the Hessian






TipWhy this works



We never actually formed the true Hessian \(\nabla^2 J\). Instead, Gauss-Newton approximates it using only first-derivative information:
\[
H \approx J^T \Sigma^{-1} J
\]
This is the whole trick behind Gauss-Newton — it gets Newton-like convergence behavior without ever paying the cost of computing second derivatives, by exploiting the specific least-squares structure of the cost function.

title: Optimization Methods
text:
4 The Full Gauss-Newton System
Summing across all factors, the actual system being solved is:
\[
\left( \sum_i J_i^T \Sigma_i^{-1} J_i \right) \delta = -\sum_i J_i^T \Sigma_i^{-1} r_i
\]
giving us the Hessian approximation:
\[
H = \sum_i J_i^T \Sigma_i^{-1} J_i
\]
Compare this to the true Hessian, which includes an extra term:
\[
\nabla^2 J = \sum_i J_i^T \Sigma_i^{-1} J_i + \sum_i r_i \nabla^2 r_i
\]
The Gauss-Newton approximation simply drops that second term.






NoteWhy this is justified



This is justified by an assumption about near convergence: as \(x \to x^*\), the residuals \(r_i \to 0\), so the dropped term \(r_i \nabla^2 r_i\) becomes negligible too. Near the solution, this approximation is excellent.



4.1 Properties of the Approximate Hessian
The approximate Hessian \(H = J^T \Sigma^{-1} J\) has two useful properties:

Symmetric
Positive semi-definite

In estimation theory, \(J^T \Sigma^{-1} J\) is also called the Information Matrix — and this connects directly back to something practically useful: the covariance of the estimated state:
\[
P \approx H^{-1} \approx \left(J^T \Sigma^{-1} J\right)^{-1}
\]
This is how SLAM systems report uncertainty on the final estimate — not just the best-guess pose, but how confident we should be in it.

title: Optimization Methods
text:
5 Why Can Gauss-Newton Fail?
The approximation only holds well near the optimum. Far from it, residuals \(r_i\) are not small, so \(r_i \nabla^2 r_i\) is no longer negligible — the approximate Hessian becomes a poor stand-in for the true one.

Gauss-Newton can diverge when initialized far from the solution.

This is the core motivation for Levenberg-Marquardt.

title: Optimization Methods
text:
6 Levenberg-Marquardt
The question LM asks: can we make the Hessian safer to invert?
Instead of solving the plain Gauss-Newton system \(H\delta = -g\), we solve a modified version:






ImportantThe LM Update



\[
(H + \lambda I)\, \delta = -g
\]
Adding \(\lambda I\) to \(H\) only changes the diagonal entries of the matrix — everything off-diagonal is untouched.



6.1 Eigenvalue Interpretation
To see why this damping term helps, suppose the Hessian has an eigen decomposition:
\[
H = V \Lambda V^T, \qquad \Lambda = \operatorname{diag}(\mu_1, \mu_2, \dots, \mu_n)
\]
Since \(VV^T = I\):
\[
H + \lambda I = V(\Lambda + \lambda I)V^T
\]
So the eigenvalues simply become \(\mu_i + \lambda\).
Why is this useful? Suppose the Hessian’s eigenvalues are \(0.0001,\ 3,\ 7,\ 15\). That tiny eigenvalue is dangerous — small eigenvalues cause instability when inverting the matrix (near-zero eigenvalues blow up in the inverse). But if we set \(\lambda = 10\):
\[
\text{eigenvalues} \to 10.0001,\ 13,\ 17,\ 25
\]
The matrix becomes well-conditioned and stable.


6.2 Another Way to See It
In Newton’s method, \(\delta = H^{-1}g\). If one eigenvalue is very small, \(\delta\) becomes huge — an unreliable, oversized step. LM prevents this by ensuring no eigenvalue of \((H + \lambda I)\) ever gets too close to zero.


6.3 LM’s Two Behaviors
LM interpolates smoothly between two extremes, depending on \(\lambda\):









Regime
What happens to \(H + \lambda I\)
Update reduces to
Behaves like




\(\lambda \gg\) every eigenvalue \(\mu_i\)
\(H + \lambda I \approx \lambda I\)
\(\delta = -\dfrac{1}{\lambda}g = -\alpha g\)
Gradient Descent


\(\lambda \to 0\)
\(H + \lambda I \to H\)
\(H\delta = -g\)
Gauss-Newton









ImportantThe elegant part



\[
\boxed{\text{Large } \lambda \to \text{Gradient Descent} \qquad \text{Small } \lambda \to \text{Gauss-Newton}}
\]
LM gets gradient descent’s safety when far from the solution, and Gauss-Newton’s speed once close to it.

title: Optimization Methods
text:
7 How to Choose \(\lambda\)?
LM adapts \(\lambda\) on the fly, iteration by iteration:

Initially, assume some starting value of \(\lambda\).
At the current cost \(J(x_k)\), compute the update \(\delta\), then the new state \(x_{new} = x_k + \delta\), and the new cost \(J(x_{new})\).
Compare the new cost to the old one, and adjust \(\lambda\) accordingly:









Outcome
Condition
Response




Good step
\(J(x_{new}) < J(x_k)\)
The quadratic approximation was accurate — decrease \(\lambda\), behave more like Gauss-Newton


Bad step
\(J(x_{new}) > J(x_k)\)
The approximation was untrustworthy — increase \(\lambda\), behave more like gradient descent









Note\(\lambda\) as a trust region



The value of \(\lambda\) effectively defines a trust region: a small \(\lambda\) means we trust the quadratic (Gauss-Newton) approximation over a larger region, while a large \(\lambda\) means we only trust it very locally.


Recall the two quantities being computed each iteration (see the full Gauss-Newton system above):
\[
H = \sum_i J_i^T \Sigma_i^{-1} J_i, \qquad g = \sum_i J_i^T \Sigma_i^{-1} r_i
\]

title: Linearization & Whitening
text:
Solving a non-linear problem numerically comes down to five repeated steps — this is the gist of Gauss-Newton / LM:

Linearize the non-linear residuals
Form a large sparse linear system
Solve for a small update \(\delta\)
Update the state
Repeat until convergence

At a higher level, the whole pipeline looks like:
Non-linear problem → Taylor expansion → linear least squares → normal equations → solve via Cholesky/QR → update state → repeat
The one idea underlying all of it: approximate the non-linear function locally with a straight line, solve that easy linear problem, then re-linearize around the new estimate and repeat. Each iteration gets closer to the true non-linear optimum.

title: Linearization & Whitening
text:
Solving a non-linear problem numerically comes down to five repeated steps — this is the gist of Gauss-Newton / LM:

Linearize the non-linear residuals
Form a large sparse linear system
Solve for a small update \(\delta\)
Update the state
Repeat until convergence

At a higher level, the whole pipeline looks like:
Non-linear problem → Taylor expansion → linear least squares → normal equations → solve via Cholesky/QR → update state → repeat
The one idea underlying all of it: approximate the non-linear function locally with a straight line, solve that easy linear problem, then re-linearize around the new estimate and repeat. Each iteration gets closer to the true non-linear optimum.

title: Linearization & Whitening
text:
2 Linearizing the Cost Function

2.1 Taylor Expansion, the Engine Behind It All
The linearization step comes straight from a first-order Taylor expansion:
\[
h_i(x_i) = h_i(x_i^0 + \delta_i) \approx h_i(x_i^0) + H_i \delta_i
\]


2.2 A Simple (Linear) Warm-Up Example
Before applying this to something genuinely non-linear, it helps to see the mechanics on something simple. Take \(f(x) = x^2\) with a current estimate \(x_0 = 2\):
\[
f(x) = f(x_0) + f'(x_0)(x - x_0) + \dots \quad \text{(ignoring higher-order terms)}
\]
\[
f(x) \approx f(x_0) + f'(x_0)(x - x_0)
\]
Substituting \(x_0 = 2\), so \(f(x_0) = 4\) and \(f'(x_0) = 4\):
\[
f(x) \approx 4 + 4(x - 2)
\]
That’s just the tangent line at \(x_0\) — a linear stand-in for the true curve, valid near \(x_0\).


2.3 The Key Mental Shift: Optimize \(\delta\), Not \(x\)
This is the part that trips people up initially: we don’t optimize \(x\) directly — we optimize \(\delta\), the correction to our current estimate:
\[
\delta = x - x_0
\]
This is easier to optimize because, near \(x_0\), the problem becomes linear in \(\delta\). Once we solve for \(\delta\), we simply update \(x \leftarrow x_0 + \delta\) and repeat the whole process at the new estimate.


2.4 The Jacobian
The matrix \(H_i\) appearing in the linearization is the Jacobian:
\[
H_i = \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial x_i} \; \text{evaluated at } x_i^0
\]






TipIntuition



Jacobian = a local sensitivity of the measurement to each unknown variable. Each entry of \(H_i\) just answers: if I nudge this one unknown a tiny bit, how much does the predicted measurement move?


Worked example: suppose the unknowns are a pose and a landmark position:
\[
x = \begin{bmatrix} x_P \\ y_P \\ \theta_P \\ x_l \\ y_l \end{bmatrix}
\qquad
h(P, l) = \operatorname{atan2}(y_l - y_P,\; x_l - x_P)
\]
Then the Jacobian is a row vector, one partial derivative per unknown:
\[
H = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x_P} & \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial y_P} & \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial \theta_P} & \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x_l} & \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial y_l} \end{bmatrix}
\]
Each column corresponds to one equation’s sensitivity to one unknown — stack these rows across all measurements and you get the full Jacobian matrix used in the linear system each iteration solves.


2.5 Substituting the Linearization Back In
Plugging the Taylor expansion back into the least squares cost:
\[
\sum_i \| z_i - h_i(x_i) \|^2 = \sum_i \| z_i - h_i(x_i^0) - H_i \delta_i \|^2
\]
The problem is now linear. This is the whole point of linearizing — the unknown \(\delta\) only appears inside the term \(H_i \delta_i\), and that’s exactly the form (\(H\delta\)) that linear algebra knows how to solve directly: a vector/matrix product.

title: Linearization & Whitening
text:
3 Whitening the Residuals
Right now each factor’s cost still carries its covariance-weighted norm:
\[
\|e\|^2_\Sigma = e^T \Sigma^{-1} e
\]
The idea behind whitening is to fold that covariance weighting directly into the residual, so we’re left with an ordinary (unweighted) squared norm — the standard form every least-squares solver expects.
Splitting \(\Sigma^{-1}\) into two symmetric square-root factors:
\[
e^T \Sigma^{-1} e = e^T \left(\Sigma^{-\frac{1}{2}}\right)^T \Sigma^{-\frac{1}{2}} e = \left(\Sigma^{-\frac{1}{2}} e\right)^T \left(\Sigma^{-\frac{1}{2}} e\right)
\]
\[
\therefore \; \|e\|^2_\Sigma = \left\| \Sigma^{-\frac{1}{2}} e \right\|^2
\]
In other words: instead of weighting the norm, we can just rescale the residual itself by \(\Sigma^{-\frac{1}{2}}\), and then take a plain Euclidean norm. That rescaling is the “whitening” — it stretches or shrinks each residual dimension according to how much we trust it, so a highly uncertain measurement contributes less to the final cost.
Applying this to our linearized residual:
\[
\left\| \Sigma_i^{-\frac{1}{2}} \left[ z_i - h_i(x_i^0) - H_i \delta_i \right] \right\|^2
\]

3.1 Defining \(A_i\) and \(b_i\)
To tidy this up, we define:
\[
A_i = \Sigma_i^{-\frac{1}{2}} H_i, \qquad b_i = \Sigma_i^{-\frac{1}{2}} \left(z_i - h_i(x_i^0)\right)
\]



Term
Meaning




\(A_i\)
the Jacobian \(H_i\), rescaled by the whitening factor


\(b_i\)
the residual at the current estimate, rescaled the same way

title: Linearization & Whitening
text:
4 The Linear System
With that substitution, the whole optimization collapses into a clean, standard linear least squares form:
\[
\delta^{*} = \operatorname*{argmin}_{\delta} \sum_i \| A_i \delta_i - b_i \|^2
\]
Stacking every factor’s \(A_i\) and \(b_i\) together gives one big, sparse linear system:
\[
A\delta = b
\]
And solving that system is the actual computational core of every SLAM backend — everything before this was about getting to this system; everything after is about solving it efficiently.








Symbol
Name
What it is




\(A\)
stacked Jacobian
every factor’s whitened sensitivity matrix, stacked together


\(b\)
stacked residual vector
every factor’s whitened residual, stacked together


\(\delta\)
state correction
what we solve for, then use to update our estimate









NoteWhy “huge” and why “sparse”




Huge — stacked across every factor, the system spans every pose and every landmark in the whole trajectory.
Sparse — most entries of \(A\) are zero. A factor only touches the handful of variables it directly constrains, so non-zero entries only appear where an actual constraint exists between variables. This is the same sparsity we saw in the factor graph structure — it’s what makes solving a system this large even feasible.

title: Linearization & Whitening
section: 5 Why Not Just Solve \(A\delta = b\) Directly?
text:
5 Why Not Just Solve \(A\delta = b\) Directly?
It’s tempting to think we’re done once we have the linear system \(A\delta = b\) — just solve it and move on. But there’s a problem:

There is usually no exact solution.

This makes sense once you think about where \(A\) and \(b\) actually come from. Different sensors give different, slightly inconsistent values — no single \(\delta\) satisfies every constraint simultaneously. So instead of solving the system exactly, we find the solution with the smallest total error.

title: Linearization & Whitening
text:
6 Setting Up the Cost
The cost we want to minimize is:
\[
J(\delta) = \|A\delta - b\|^2
\]
Expanding this out:
\[
J(\delta) = (A\delta - b)^T (A\delta - b) = (\delta^T A^T - b^T)(A\delta - b)
\]
\[
J(\delta) = \delta^T A^T A \delta - 2\delta^T A^T b + b^T b
\]
This is now a quadratic function — the matrix version of the familiar \(ax^2 + bx + c\) from single-variable calculus.

title: Linearization & Whitening
text:
7 Minimizing the Cost
For a quadratic, minimizing is straightforward: take the derivative and set it to zero. Differentiating \(J(\delta)\) with respect to \(\delta\) and setting the result to zero gives:
\[
A^T A \, \delta = A^T b
\]






ImportantThe Normal Equations



\[
A^T A \, \delta = A^T b
\]
This is the linear system that actually gets solved at every iteration of the optimizer. Everything up to this point — linearization, whitening, stacking factors — exists to produce this one equation.

title: Linearization & Whitening
text:
8 Why “Normal”?
The name isn’t arbitrary — it comes directly from geometry.
Picture every column of \(A\) as spanning some subspace. Since \(b\) generally doesn’t lie exactly inside that column space (that’s exactly why there’s no exact solution to \(A\delta = b\)), the least squares solution instead projects \(b\) onto the column space — finding the closest point in that space to \(b\).
That leaves an error vector:
\[
r = b - A\delta
\]






NoteThe geometric picture



At the optimal \(\delta\), the error vector \(r\) becomes perpendicular (normal) to the column space of \(A\). That perpendicularity condition is exactly what the normal equations enforce — which is where they get their name.

title: Linearization & Whitening
text:
9 The Information Matrix
We define:
\[
\Lambda = A^T A
\]
where \(\Lambda\) is called the information matrix, also known as the Hessian of the cost function.
\(A^T A\) has two properties that matter a great deal for how we solve the system:








Property
Statement
Why it matters




Symmetric
\(\Lambda^T = \Lambda\)
halves the storage and work needed to represent \(\Lambda\)


Positive semi-definite
\(x^T A^T A x \geq 0\) for all \(x\)
guarantees the quadratic cost has a well-defined minimum, not a saddle point



That positive semi-definiteness is what makes the system well-behaved enough to allow Cholesky factorization — a fast, numerically stable way to solve the normal equations.






WarningApproximation, not the true Hessian



The true Hessian of the cost function is \(\dfrac{\partial^2 J}{\partial x^2}\), but in practice we approximate it with \(A^T A\).
This approximation is exactly what separates Gauss-Newton from full Newton’s method: Gauss-Newton never computes second derivatives directly. That saves a lot of computation, at the cost of \(A^T A\) only being a good approximation of the true Hessian near the solution.

title: Visualizing Factor Graphs
text:
We’re solving for a state vector containing both robot poses and landmarks:
\[
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} P_1 \\ P_2 \\ P_3 \\ l_1 \\ l_2 \end{bmatrix}
\]
where \(P_i\) are robot poses at different times and \(l_i\) are landmark positions.

title: Visualizing Factor Graphs
text:
We’re solving for a state vector containing both robot poses and landmarks:
\[
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} P_1 \\ P_2 \\ P_3 \\ l_1 \\ l_2 \end{bmatrix}
\]
where \(P_i\) are robot poses at different times and \(l_i\) are landmark positions.

title: Visualizing Factor Graphs
text:
2 Known vs. unknown
The measurements are what’s “known” — but here’s the key idea that trips people up at first: every measurement carries uncertainty. A LiDAR scan doesn’t tell you “the wall is exactly 3.000m away.” It tells you “the wall is probably around 3m away, plus or minus some noise.”
That uncertainty is why SLAM is fundamentally a probability problem, not a geometry problem.

The robot never knows — it only believes.

This isn’t just a nice turn of phrase — it’s the mathematical foundation of the whole field. The robot maintains a belief: a probability distribution over where it might be, not a single certain answer.

title: Visualizing Factor Graphs
section: 3 Why probabilities must sum to one
text:
3 Why probabilities must sum to one
\[
\int p(x)\,dx = 1
\]
This says: all possibilities sum to 1. In plain terms — the robot must exist somewhere. Its belief might be spread out and uncertain, but that uncertainty is distributed over the full space of possibilities, and it always adds up to total certainty that some pose is the true one.
This single constraint is what turns “the robot is confused about its position” into something we can actually compute with.

title: Visualizing Factor Graphs
text:
4 The State Vector
Everything SLAM is trying to solve for lives in one big state vector, stacking robot poses and landmark positions together:
\[
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} P_1 \\ P_2 \\ P_3 \\ l_1 \\ l_2 \end{bmatrix}
\rightarrow
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ y_1 \\ \theta_1 \\ x_2 \\ y_2 \end{bmatrix}
\]
Each pose is \([x, y, \theta]\), and each landmark is \([x, y]\). Note that this is a vector, not a matrix — a flat list of all the unknowns concatenated together.
This matters because it tells you how the optimization actually works: it doesn’t solve for the robot’s path first and then the map, or vice versa. It solves for every unknown at once, jointly.

title: Visualizing Factor Graphs
text:
5 The Posterior Distribution
The quantity we actually care about is:
\[
p(x \mid z) \Rightarrow P(\text{pose} \mid \text{measurements})
\]
In words: given everything the sensors have measured, what’s the probability distribution over where the robot actually is (and where the landmarks actually are)?

title: Visualizing Factor Graphs
text:
6 Why Factor Graphs?
The core idea is deceptively simple: draw constraints as nodes. Instead of writing one huge joint probability expression, we represent each piece of evidence — each measurement, each motion step — as its own node in a graph. This turns an intractable-looking equation into something structured and sparse.

title: Visualizing Factor Graphs
text:
7 Bayesian Networks
Bayesian networks represent joint probability — but the trick is they don’t store it as one giant function. Instead, they exploit conditional independence to break it into small, manageable pieces.
For example, take three variables \(a\), \(b\), \(c\). If the dependency chain is \(A \to B \to C\), then instead of needing the full joint distribution, we can factor it as:
\[
P(a,b,c) = P(a) \cdot P(b \mid a) \cdot P(c \mid b)
\]
This is the whole reason factor graphs are tractable at all — most variables in SLAM don’t directly depend on most other variables, so we never need to compute anything close to the full joint distribution.

7.1 Applying this to our state vector
The same logic applies directly to \(p(x \mid z)\). By Bayes’ rule:
\[
p(x \mid z) \propto p(z \mid x) \cdot p(x)
\]
Expanding over all our poses, landmarks, and measurements:
\[
\begin{aligned}
p(x \mid z) \;\propto\;\; & p(z_1,z_2,z_3,z_4 \mid P_1,P_2,P_3,l_1,l_2) \\
& \cdot\, p(P_1,P_2,P_3,l_1,l_2)
\end{aligned}
\]
Given the dependency structure — where \(P_1 \to P_2 \to P_3\) form the trajectory, and \(l_1, l_2\) are landmarks observed at different points along it — this factors into:
\[
\begin{aligned}
p(x \mid z) \;\propto\;\; & p(P_1) \cdot p(l_1) \cdot p(l_2) \\
& \cdot\, p(P_2\mid P_1) \cdot p(P_3\mid P_2) \\
& \cdot\, p(z_1\mid P_1) \cdot p(z_2\mid P_1,l_1) \\
& \cdot\, p(z_3\mid P_2,l_1) \cdot p(z_4\mid P_3,l_2)
\end{aligned}
\]
Every term in that product corresponds to one measurement or one motion step. Nothing here is arbitrary — it falls directly out of which variables were involved in producing which observation.


7.2 From Bayesian network to factor graph
Here’s the structure before and after the transformation:
Bayesian network (directed, showing dependency direction):



Bayesian network for SLAM: poses P1, P2, P3 in a chain, landmarks l1 and l2, and measurements z1 through z4


Factor graph (undirected, showing which variables a constraint touches):



Factor graph for SLAM: variable nodes P1, P2, P3, l1, l2 connected to factor nodes φ1 through φ9



Unary factors (priors): \(\phi_1(P_1)\), \(\phi_4(l_1)\), \(\phi_5(l_2)\)
Motion factors: \(\phi_2(P_2,P_1)\), \(\phi_3(P_3,P_2)\)
Observation factors: \(\phi_6(P_1)\), \(\phi_7(P_1,l_1)\), \(\phi_8(P_2,l_1)\), \(\phi_9(P_3,l_2)\)

The shift from Bayesian network to factor graph is really a shift in what the nodes mean. In the Bayesian network, arrows encode causal or conditional direction. In the factor graph, we drop the directionality and instead ask: which variables does this one constraint touch?

title: Visualizing Factor Graphs
section: 8 What a Factor Graph Actually Is
text:
8 What a Factor Graph Actually Is

A factor graph is a graph that represents the factorization of a function.

A factor is just a function that says how compatible a set of variables is with one measurement. Every factor corresponds to one constraint — one measurement, one prior, one motion estimate.
The graph has exactly two kinds of nodes:

Variable nodes (circles) — poses and landmarks, the things we’re solving for
Factor nodes (squares) — constraints, the evidence tying variables together

Because edges only ever connect a variable node to a factor node (never variable-to-variable or factor-to-factor), this makes it a bipartite graph. That bipartite structure is what keeps the whole system sparse and efficient to optimize — most variables only touch a handful of factors, not all of them.

title: Visualizing Factor Graphs
text:
9 Types of Factors
Two flavors of constraint show up constantly in a factor graph:

\(\phi(P_1)\) — a unary constraint, or prior. It involves just one variable, expressing a belief about it in isolation (e.g., “the robot starts near the origin”).
\(\phi(P_2, P_1)\) — a binary constraint, or between factor. It ties two variables together (e.g., “the robot moved this much between these two poses”).

title: Visualizing Factor Graphs
text:
10 Why Sparsity Matters
Here’s the property that makes factor graphs practical at scale: they’re sparse. Most nodes never connect to each other. A landmark observed early in a trajectory has no direct relationship to a pose visited much later — they only become linked indirectly, through the chain of poses in between. This makes sparsity one of the most important properties of the whole system.
Formally, a factor graph is a tuple:
\[
F = (U, V, E)
\]
where \(U\) = factor nodes, \(V\) = variable nodes, and \(E\) = edges. The whole factorized function is just the product over all factors:
\[
\phi(x) = \prod_i \phi_i(x_i)
\]
This sparsity is why SLAM problems with thousands of poses and landmarks are still solvable in real time — the underlying linear systems end up being sparse matrices, and sparse solvers scale far better than dense ones.

title: Visualizing Factor Graphs
text:
11 Why We Need Iteration
Non-linear equations can’t be solved in one shot the way linear ones can. There’s no closed-form inverse waiting for us. So instead, we reach for iterative algorithms like Gauss-Newton or Levenberg-Marquardt (LM).
It’s worth sitting with this idea before diving into the mechanics:

No individual factor solves the problem. The solution comes from the collective agreement of all factors.

Every factor pulls the estimate toward what it believes is true, and the final solution is wherever all of these competing pulls settle into balance.
